doc.title: Closing the books — reset the scoreboard
doc.event: Dec 31, after adjustments: Service Revenue shows $48,000, total expenses $31,000, owner draws $8,000. Close the books with the four closing entries and prove Income Summary does its job.

variants.label: Four entries, one purpose: zero the temporary accounts into capital

lines.label: zero it out
lines.why: Revenue is credit-normal, so closing it takes a DEBIT for its full balance — the account resets to zero for next year.
lines.trap: Temporary accounts (revenues, expenses, draws) measure ONE period; permanent accounts (A, L, capital) run forever. Closing only touches the temporaries.

lines.label: collect the credits
lines.why: A one-day scratchpad account that exists only to net income together.
lines.trap: Income Summary never appears on any financial statement — born and buried on closing day.

lines.label: collect the debits
lines.why: After entries 1–2, Income Summary's balance IS net income: 48,000 cr − 31,000 dr = 17,000 credit.
lines.trap: That 17,000 matching the income statement is your built-in check — a mismatch means an account was missed.

lines.label: zero them out
lines.why: Each expense (debit-normal) is credited for its balance — in practice one compound entry crediting every expense account.
lines.trap: Every single expense account must appear; the one you forget stays open and poisons next year.

lines.label: empty the scratchpad
lines.why: The netted income moves home to the owner's claim.
lines.trap: A net LOSS flips this entry (credit Income Summary, debit Capital) — direction follows the balance.

lines.label: earnings become equity
lines.why: This entry IS the relay from Ch. 6 written in journal form: net income flowing into capital.
lines.trap: Corporations do the identical move into Retained Earnings — same machine, different label.

lines.label: capital shrinks
lines.why: Draws reduce the owner's claim directly.
lines.trap: Draws close to CAPITAL, never through Income Summary — they are not an expense and never touch income.

lines.label: zero it out
lines.why: Capital now reads 10,000 + 17,000 − 8,000 = 19,000 — exactly Ch. 6's ending capital.
lines.trap: After closing, ONLY balance-sheet accounts have balances. That's the definition of ready-for-next-year.

computationPaths.narration: Where closing sits — the full accounting cycle, the loop every business runs every period:

steps.label: During the period
steps.formulaText: 1 analyze transactions → 2 journalize → 3 post to ledger

steps.label: At period end
steps.formulaText: 4 trial balance → 5 adjusting entries → 6 adjusted trial balance

steps.label: Report & reset
steps.formulaText: 7 financial statements → 8 closing entries → 9 post-closing trial balance

memorize.body: Four closes, in order: (1) revenues → Income Summary, (2) expenses → Income Summary, (3) Income Summary (= net income) → Capital, (4) Draws → Capital. Temporaries hit zero; Capital catches everything.
Temporary vs permanent: 'does this account measure THIS period or the whole life?' Period → close it. Life → leave it.
Post-closing trial balance contains ONLY assets, liabilities, and capital. If a revenue or expense shows up on it, a closing entry was missed.

questions.prompt: After closing revenues (48,000) and expenses (31,000), Income Summary's balance before entry 3 is…

distractors.feedback: 48,000 is only the revenue side — Income Summary nets BOTH closes: 48,000 credit − 31,000 debit = 17,000 credit, which is net income. That equality is the point of the account.

build.scaffold: March the four in order and check the milestone: after entry 2, Income Summary must equal net income. Draws take the side door (entry 4) — never through the summary.
